name: Panemunė Castle
region: Jurbarkas District, Nemunas River, near Smalininkai
architectural_style: Renaissance-Baroque nobleman's residence built 1604 by Dutch architect Peter Nonhardt, over the site of the Teutonic Order's Christmemel fortress; the building's main block combines Renaissance structural proportions with Baroque ornamental elements added in later phases, particularly the 1759-1783 renovations by Antanas Anupras Gelgaudas; subsequent Neo-Classical refinements reflect the Classicist taste of the late 18th century; the overall character is that of a relatively intimate Polish-Lithuanian nobleman's country seat rather than a grand palace — a scale appropriate to the provincial nobility of the western Lithuanian borderland; the castle is now operated by the Vilnius Academy of Arts as an arts and culture venue

opening_hours.seasonal_note: Open primarily during the main tourist season (May–September); the castle is operated by the Vilnius Academy of Arts, which may host exhibitions, artist residencies, and cultural events that affect access. Confirm current opening schedule and any event programming via the Vilnius Academy of Arts website or the Jurbarkas tourism portal before visiting. The GYG Nemunas River Castles tour from Vilnius (t1402021, from $227, 10 hours, max 7 guests) includes a dedicated guided stop at Panemunė.

visit_duration: 1–1.5 hours (castle and grounds); 10 hours (GYG full Nemunas River Castles circuit from Vilnius)
best_season: May to September — the castle's riverside position on the lower Nemunas is most accessible in summer; the Vilnius Academy of Arts programming tends to be concentrated in the warmer months
accessibility: The castle exterior and surrounding grounds are accessible at ground level. Interior accessibility depends on the current exhibition layout and the Vilnius Academy of Arts' programming. The riverside position involves some uneven terrain on approach.
tagline: A Dutch architect's 1604 Baroque manor on the Teutonic frontier — its last private owner commanded the November Uprising against Russia and the Tsarist authorities took it the day he died
highlights: The Teutonic Christmemel (1313) — the site's first recorded structure was the Teutonic Order's Christmemel fortress, established in 1313 at the mouth of the Jūra River where it enters the Nemunas; it was captured the following year (1314) by Grand Duke Vytenis of Lithuania, one of the earliest documented major Lithuanian military victories against the Teutonic Order in the lower Nemunas region

Built 1604 by Dutch architect Peter Nonhardt — the Renaissance-Baroque manor house that replaced the Teutonic military installation was designed by Peter Nonhardt, a Dutch-born architect working in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth; Nonhardt represents the international architectural exchange that characterized the Commonwealth's cultural life in the early 17th century, bringing Western European building practices to the Lithuanian frontier

Antanas Gelgaudas and the November Uprising (1830–1831) — the castle's most historically consequential owner was Antanas Anupras Gelgaudas, Elder of Samogitia, who acquired it in 1759 and undertook major renovations; his descendant (and near-namesake) Antanas Gelgaudas led the November Uprising of 1830–1831 as commander-in-chief of the Lithuanian forces fighting for independence from Tsarist Russia; when Gelgaudas died fighting, the Tsarist authorities confiscated the estate immediately, transferring it to Russian imperial ownership — a punitive sequestration that was standard practice against families associated with the uprising

Vilnius Academy of Arts — since Lithuanian independence in 1990, Panemunė Castle has been managed by the Vilnius Academy of Arts as an arts venue, hosting exhibitions, artist residencies, and cultural events; the transformation from a Tsarist-confiscated noble estate to a contemporary arts institution is characteristic of many Lithuanian manor houses that survived Soviet-era institutional use

The westernmost castle on the Nemunas circuit — Panemunė Castle is the furthest downstream of the four Nemunas River Castles, positioned where the river begins its final approach to the Curonian Lagoon and the Baltic Sea; the surrounding landscape is the flat, river-meadow terrain of the lower Nemunas, quite different from the hillier country around Kaunas

GYG Kaunas & Nemunas River Castles day tour from Vilnius (t1402021, from $227, max 7 guests, 10 hours) — includes a dedicated guided stop at Panemunė Castle alongside Kaunas Castle, Raudondvaris Manor, and Raudonė Castle
description:
Panemunė Castle sits at the far western end of the Nemunas River Castles chain, on the flat riverside terrain of the lower Nemunas near the mouth of the Jūra tributary — the furthest downstream of the four fortifications that mark the Grand Duchy of Lithuania's principal river frontier with the Teutonic Order. The site's history begins earlier than the castle itself: in 1313, the Teutonic Order established the fortress of Christmemel here, at the confluence of the Jūra and the Nemunas, as part of the network of military positions that gave the Order control of the lower river system. In 1314, Grand Duke Vytenis of Lithuania captured Christmemel — one of the earliest major Lithuanian victories over the Teutonic Order documented in the written record, and one that established the strategic importance of this particular river junction in the century of conflict that followed.

The military installations of the Teutonic period were superseded in 1604, when the site's then-owners commissioned a new building designed by Peter Nonhardt, a Dutch-born architect working in the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth. Nonhardt represents the international cultural and architectural exchange that characterized the Commonwealth at the height of its confidence: architects, artists, and craftsmen from Western and Central Europe brought their building traditions into the Baltic and Lithuanian regions, creating a hybrid architectural culture that expressed the Commonwealth's cosmopolitan aspirations. The manor house Nonhardt built at Panemunė was a Renaissance-Baroque structure appropriate to the scale and taste of the Lithuanian provincial nobility — neither the grand palatial ambition of the Vilnius magnates nor the modest timber building of the rural gentry, but a comfortable, ornamental residence that signalled the owner's status without the resources of the truly great Lithuanian families.

The most consequential period of the castle's history begins with Antanas Anupras Gelgaudas, Elder of Samogitia, who acquired Panemunė in 1759 and undertook a comprehensive renovation that introduced Baroque and Neo-Classical elements into the existing 17th-century structure. Gelgaudas was one of the most powerful men in western Lithuania — the Elder of Samogitia was a major administrative and social position in the Grand Duchy's provincial hierarchy — and his investment in Panemunė reflected both his personal taste and his desire to establish the castle as an appropriate seat for his family.

The castle's most dramatically consequential historical moment came not through the building itself but through the political choices of a subsequent family member bearing nearly the same name. Antanas Gelgaudas — the Elder's descendant and near-namesake — became commander-in-chief of the Lithuanian forces in the November Uprising of 1830–1831. The November Uprising was the largest armed resistance to Tsarist Russian rule in the territories of the former Grand Duchy of Lithuania and the former Kingdom of Poland — a coordinated insurrection that sought to restore the pre-partition Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth by force of arms. The uprising ultimately failed: the Tsarist military suppressed it after months of fighting, and the consequences for the families of those who had led it were severe. When Antanas Gelgaudas died fighting in the course of the uprising — in some sources at Šiauliai in 1831 — the Tsarist authorities confiscated the Panemunė estate as a punitive sequestration, a standard practice applied against families associated with the uprising throughout the former Lithuanian and Polish territories. The castle passed into Russian imperial ownership and did not return to Lithuanian hands until after independence.

The Soviet period saw the castle used for institutional purposes — an experience shared by most Lithuanian manor houses that survived the 20th century without complete demolition or abandonment. Since Lithuanian independence in 1990, Panemunė Castle has been managed by the Vilnius Academy of Arts, Lithuania's principal art and design university, which operates it as a venue for exhibitions, artist residencies, summer schools, and cultural events. The transformation from Tsarist-confiscated noble estate to contemporary arts institution gives the castle a current use that is both culturally appropriate and practically sustainable — a pattern successfully replicated with other Lithuanian manor houses that found institutional patrons after 1990.

The castle is the final stop on the Nemunas River Castles circuit. [Raudonė Castle](/castles/lithuania/raudone-castle), 30 kilometres upstream, preserves the 34-metre cylindrical tower of the Teutonic Order's Bayersburg as a climbable observation platform. [Raudondvaris Manor](/castles/lithuania/raudondvaris-manor), 80 kilometres upstream, was Queen Barbara Radziwiłł's red-brick manor before the Tyszkiewicz family filled it with old masters. [Kaunas Castle](/castles/lithuania/kaunas-castle), at the Nemunas-Neris confluence 100 kilometres upstream, is the first brick castle of the Grand Duchy of Lithuania.

For the broader Lithuanian castle network: [Trakai Island Castle](/castles/lithuania/trakai-island-castle) in the lake district northeast of Vilnius is the most visually striking medieval castle in Lithuania. [Gediminas Tower](/castles/lithuania/gediminas-tower) in Vilnius is the upper castle of the Grand Duchy's capital.
history: 1313: Teutonic Order establishes Christmemel fortress at the Jūra-Nemunas confluence. 1314: Grand Duke Vytenis of Lithuania captures Christmemel — an early major Lithuanian victory over the Teutonic Order. 1410: Battle of Grunwald ends Teutonic Order military power; the site remains in Lithuanian control. 1597: Site acquired by Jonuš Eperješ. 1604: Renaissance-Baroque manor house built by Dutch architect Peter Nonhardt — the building that constitutes the current castle. 1759: Antanas Anupras Gelgaudas (Elder of Samogitia) acquires the castle and begins renovation. 1783: Major Baroque and Neo-Classical renovation completed. 1830–1831: November Uprising against Russia — Antanas Gelgaudas serves as Lithuanian commander-in-chief. 1831: Gelgaudas dies fighting; Tsarist authorities confiscate the Panemunė estate as punitive sequestration; castle passes to Russian imperial ownership. 19th century: Under Russian administration. Soviet period: Castle used for institutional purposes. 1990: Lithuanian independence; castle transferred to Vilnius Academy of Arts. Present day: Operated as an arts venue by the Vilnius Academy of Arts.
how_to_visit:
**Direct visit:** The castle is near Smalininkai in Jurbarkas District, accessible by car from Jurbarkas (approximately 20km). Confirm current opening hours and any scheduled events with the Vilnius Academy of Arts website before visiting.

**GYG Nemunas River Castles day tour from Vilnius (~$227, t1402021, 10 hours, max 7 guests):** Includes a dedicated guided stop at Panemunė Castle alongside the other three Nemunas valley sites. Departs from Vilnius; lunch included. This is the easiest way to see all four castles in a single day without a rental car.

**Combine with:** Raudonė Castle (30km upstream), Raudondvaris Manor (80km upstream), Kaunas Castle (100km upstream).

faqs.question: Why did the Tsarist authorities take the castle?
faqs.answer: Antanas Gelgaudas, whose family owned Panemunė Castle, led the Lithuanian forces in the November Uprising of 1830–1831 — the largest armed insurrection against Russian rule in the former Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth territories. When the uprising was suppressed and Gelgaudas died fighting in 1831, the Tsarist authorities confiscated the Panemunė estate as a punitive measure. This was standard practice across the former Commonwealth: families identified as leaders or significant participants in the uprising had their estates sequestered and transferred to Russian imperial ownership. The Gelgaudas family did not recover the property.

faqs.question: Who runs the castle now and can you visit?
faqs.answer: Panemunė Castle has been managed by the Vilnius Academy of Arts — Lithuania's principal art and design university — since Lithuanian independence in 1990. The Academy uses it as an exhibition and residency venue, which means the schedule of events and openings varies by season. The castle is generally open to visitors in the summer season (May–September), but confirm current hours and programming via the Vilnius Academy of Arts website or the Jurbarkas District tourism portal before making a special trip.

faqs.question: What was the Teutonic Order's Christmemel and why was it here?
faqs.answer: Christmemel was a Teutonic Order fortress established in 1313 at the confluence of the Jūra River and the Nemunas — the same strategic principle that governed the placement of Kaunas Castle at the Nemunas-Neris confluence, applied here to the lower river system. Control of the Jūra mouth gave the Teutonic Order a position on the Nemunas that allowed them to monitor and interdict Lithuanian movement along the river. Grand Duke Vytenis's capture of Christmemel in 1314 is one of the earliest recorded Lithuanian military successes against the Teutonic Order, and it established this section of the lower Nemunas as a contested frontier for the century of conflict that followed.
